Review 5 cards in 5 minutes at the start of your day. That’s enough to keep key papers accessible. This integrates learning science while keeping the system usable.
How do you reconcile Pomodoro with deep work approaches that recommend long uninterrupted blocks? Are these just different philosophies or can they be combined coherently?

They can be combined coherently by treating “Pomodoro” as a general timeboxing framework rather than a fixed 25/5 dogma. A coherent hybrid model: - Use longer deep work blocks (60–120 minutes) for tasks with high integration demands (drafting, complex synthesis). - Use Pomodoro-style shorter cycles (20–35 minutes) for tasks where frequent checkpoints help (editing, literature scanning, data extraction). - Keep the “Pomodoro principles”: clear intention, single-tasking, planned breaks, and closure—regardless of interval length. Think of it as two layers: 1) Macro-block: a 90-minute deep block reserved for one project. 2) Micro-structure inside: you might still do 2×45 minutes with a 5-minute reset in the middle, or even 3×30 minutes with brief pauses—but you don’t switch tasks. This respects deep work’s emphasis on immersion while still using breaks to manage time-on-task effects and maintain quality.
Are there any studies that compare different break schedules head-to-head? Like frequent short breaks versus fewer long breaks? What do we know about that tradeoff?

There are studies in ergonomics and occupational psychology that compare micro-break schedules (very short breaks more frequently) to less frequent longer breaks, often in contexts like computer work, repetitive tasks, or monitoring. General findings tend to be: - Frequent short breaks can reduce fatigue and discomfort and maintain performance, especially for tasks with physical strain (typing, repetitive motion) or monotony. - For complex cognitive tasks, the picture is mixed: frequent breaks can help prevent fatigue but may increase disruption and reorientation time. - Total break time matters: if two schedules have equal total break time, distribution can still matter depending on whether recovery is immediate (benefits from frequent breaks) or requires longer disengagement (benefits from longer breaks). A useful way to think about the tradeoff: - Recovery curve: some recovery happens quickly (seconds to a couple minutes), but deeper recovery may require longer. - Re-entry cost: each break has a fixed overhead (reorient, recall goals). More breaks means more overhead. So you want a schedule where marginal recovery benefit exceeds marginal re-entry cost. Practical implication: - If you notice that after 30–40 minutes your reading comprehension drops, take a short break then. - If you notice that it takes you 3–5 minutes to re-enter a writing task after any break, reduce break frequency and instead take fewer, longer breaks. You can measure this in your self-experiment: track re-entry time and output quality by interval number (first interval vs third). That shows where fatigue begins to dominate.
Let’s get more technical: what exactly is “vigilance decrement”? How is it measured, and does it apply to writing as well as to monitoring tasks?

Vigilance decrement is the decline in performance over time during tasks that require sustained attention, especially when critical events are infrequent or the task is monotonous. Classic vigilance tasks involve monitoring a stream of stimuli for rare targets; performance typically drops over 20–40 minutes, sometimes sooner. Measurement: - Hit rate (detecting targets) - False alarms - Reaction time - Signal detection metrics (sensitivity d’, criterion) Mechanisms proposed include: - Resource theories: sustained attention consumes limited cognitive resources. - Mind-wandering: under-stimulation leads to attentional lapses. - Motivational/control allocation: people reduce effort when the task’s subjective value decreases. Does it apply to writing? Not in the same direct measurement sense (there aren’t “rare targets”), but similar time-on-task effects occur: - Error rates increase (typos, logical inconsistencies) - Coherence declines (topic drift) - Speed changes (slower sentence formation or, conversely, faster but sloppier output) - Metacognitive monitoring worsens (less noticing of weak arguments) You can operationalize “vigilance-like decrement” in writing by tracking: - number of corrections needed later per 500 words - coherence ratings (self or peer) by paragraph position - time to produce each 100-word chunk across a long session Breaks can counteract these decrements by restoring alertness and control, though the effect size will depend on the writer and the task.
Some people recommend “52/17” or “90/20” schedules. Are those grounded in anything, or are they just internet folklore?

Those schedules are typically based on observational analyses (e.g., time-tracking data) or interpretations of biological rhythm ideas, rather than controlled experiments establishing that 52/17 is universally optimal. - 52/17: Often traced to analyses of productivity app data, where high performers tended to work ~52 minutes then break ~17. That’s correlational: it might reflect the types of tasks those users do or how they self-select. - 90/20: Sometimes linked to ultradian rhythm claims. There are physiological cycles, but mapping them directly to cognitive performance schedules is not strongly established in a prescriptive way. What these schedules do capture: - Many people benefit from longer focus blocks than 25 minutes for complex work. - Longer breaks (15–20 minutes) can be more genuinely restorative than 5 minutes, especially if you move and change context. A reasonable evidence-seeking stance: - Treat 52/17 and 90/20 as hypotheses. - Test them against 25/5 and 45/10 using your metrics. - Choose the schedule that maximizes output quality per unit time and is sustainable.
I worry about the timer making me rush. How do I prevent the interval from turning into “speed over quality,” especially for writing?

Three adjustments help keep quality high: 1) Define quality constraints per interval Instead of “write as much as possible,” set a micro-goal like: - “Draft this subsection with 3 claims, each supported by one citation placeholder.” - “Write the argument chain in bullet form first, then expand.” This shifts the timer from speed pressure to structure pressure. 2) Separate drafting and editing explicitly Use different modes: - Drafting intervals: no line-editing, placeholders allowed. - Editing intervals: shorter, focused on clarity, citations, and coherence. This reduces the temptation to polish under time pressure. 3) Use the timer as a stop signal, not a speed signal The purpose is to create a checkpoint, not a race. You can even use a timer that doesn’t show the countdown (only an alarm at the end) to reduce “clock watching.” Also, end each interval with a quick quality check: - “What is the main claim of what I just wrote?” - “Does each paragraph have a topic sentence?” That keeps standards intact.
Okay, suppose I adopt a customized Pomodoro: 45/10 for drafting, 30/5 for reading, with closure rituals. How long should I stick with it before deciding it works? And what would count as “works”?

Give it enough time to get past initial novelty and to cover different task days. A practical evaluation window is 2–4 weeks. A good decision rule: 1) Define “works” in advance For writing: - Output: ≥10% increase in drafted words per 60 work minutes (or sections completed) - Quality: no increase in next-day revision intensity (or a stable/improved rubric score) - Sustainability: you follow the schedule on ≥80% of planned sessions without feeling compelled to abandon it mid-block For reading: - Comprehension: stable or improved quiz/free recall scores - Extraction: higher completion rate of templates and better ability to cite papers accurately later 2) Collect enough sessions Aim for ~10–15 sessions per condition if you’re comparing schedules. If you’re just evaluating one schedule against your baseline, log at least 10 sessions before judging. 3) Use a baseline Even a one-week baseline of your current approach helps. Otherwise you’ll compare to a vague memory. 4) Look for consistent direction, not perfection If most sessions show better output with equal quality, that’s meaningful even if a few sessions are off. 5) Check failure modes If it “works” but you notice: - re-entry time is high - breaks are drifting into distractions then the schedule may be fine but the break protocol needs tightening.
Can you give me a concrete template for logging sessions? Something I can paste into a notes app and fill quickly.

Yes—here’s a compact template: Session Log Date: Start time: Task type: (Reading / Extraction / Synthesis / Drafting / Editing) Project: Condition: (30/5, 45/10, etc.) Planned work minutes: Actual work minutes: Break compliance: (Y/N; if N, what happened?) Micro-goal: Output metric: (words / sections / pages / templates completed) Closure done: (Y/N) Re-entry time after breaks (avg, min): Self-rating (optional): focus 1–7, clarity 1–7 Next-day Quality (fill next day) Revision intensity: (Light / Medium / Heavy) Rubric score (0–3 each): clarity __, coherence __, evidence __ Notes: (1–2 lines) If you keep it this short, it’s feasible. The “Next-day Quality” is important because same-day quality ratings are often unreliable.

One more thing: sometimes I’m doing a task that is inherently “chunky,” like outlining a section that might take 70 minutes. If the timer goes off mid-chunk, should I stop anyway? Or is it better to finish the chunk?

For chunky tasks, it’s usually better to stop at natural boundaries rather than obey the timer rigidly. Think of the timer as a prompt to check in, not a hard stop. A boundary-based rule: - When the timer rings, ask: “Am I within ~3–7 minutes of a clean boundary?” - If yes: finish to the boundary, then stop and do closure. - If no: stop anyway, do a 30-second closure note (“I’m mid-step; next is ___”), then break. This approach balances: - minimizing interruption costs (finish a sub-step) - preventing runaway sessions (you still break) - preserving the integrity of your mental model In your logs, note when you “overran to boundary” so you can see whether that improves re-entry and quality.
How does all of this relate to procrastination? Not in the clinical sense, just the everyday tendency to delay. Is Pomodoro mainly an anti-delay tool?

Pomodoro often functions as an anti-delay tool because it reduces initiation barriers and ambiguity. Mechanisms: - Low commitment: “Just 25 minutes” feels smaller than “finish the report.” - Clear next action: you decide what to do for the interval, reducing choice paralysis. - Immediate endpoint: makes effort feel bounded. - Feedback loop: frequent completions provide reinforcement. But it’s not only about delay. For people who already start easily, Pomodoro’s value is more about sustaining quality, managing fatigue, and creating checkpoints. If your main issue is delaying the start, emphasize: - very short first interval (10–15 minutes) - extremely concrete micro-goal (“write the heading and first bullet list”) - immediate closure and plan for the next interval If your main issue is maintaining coherence and depth, emphasize: - longer intervals - boundary-based stopping - stronger closure rituals

I want to keep the conversation evidence-focused. Can you summarize the strongest empirical claims we can reasonably make about timed work intervals and breaks, and the weaker claims that are more speculative?

Stronger, more defensible claims: 1) Performance often declines with time-on-task on sustained cognitive tasks; breaks can restore performance, especially for vigilance-like demands. 2) Task switching and interruptions impose measurable costs; minimizing disruptive break activities can improve re-entry and performance. 3) Timeboxing can improve follow-through by reducing planning overhead and creating near-term goals and feedback. 4) Breaks that reduce engagement with the same cognitive resources (e.g., moving, visual rest) are often more restorative than breaks filled with new demanding content. Moderate claims (context-dependent but plausible): 1) The optimal work/break ratio varies by task complexity and individual differences; longer blocks often suit integrative work, shorter blocks suit discrete tasks. 2) Closure rituals reduce re-entry cost by externalizing goals and context. 3) Light physical activity breaks can improve subjective energy and sometimes performance. Weaker/speculative claims: 1) Specific “magic” schedules like 25/5, 52/17, or 90/20 being universally optimal. 2) Strict ultradian rhythm prescriptions as the main explanation for best interval lengths. 3) Any strong claim that Pomodoro uniquely boosts creativity or uniquely harms it; it depends heavily on how it’s implemented and what phase of creative work you’re in. This is why a personalized, metric-based approach is appropriate: the general principles are supported, but the exact parameter settings are best tuned empirically for your tasks.
This has been helpful. If you had to give me a short “starter protocol” for the next week that’s research-informed but not overcomplicated, what would it be?

One-week starter protocol (simple, research-informed): Intervals - Reading: 35 work / 5 break - Drafting: 50 work / 10 break - Editing: 25 work / 5 break Work rules - Each interval starts with a micro-goal written in one sentence. - Single-task only. - Last 2 minutes: closure ritual (next-action line + 2 context bullets). Break rules - No new information input. - Stand/move + water + look far away. - Optional: 5 slow breaths. Daily plan (3 hours) - 1× Reading block (35/5 + 25/5 if you have time) - 1× Drafting block (50/10) - 1× Editing or synthesis block (25/5 or 50/10 depending) Metrics (minimal) - Words drafted per work hour - One next-day quality rating: Light/Medium/Heavy revision - Break compliance (Y/N) At the end of the week, decide one adjustment: either interval length or break type—change only one variable at a time.
Great. Last question: if I’m skeptical by nature, what’s the most honest way to think about Pomodoro? Like, what is it really doing?

The most honest framing is: Pomodoro is a behavioral scaffold. It doesn’t unlock hidden brain modes; it packages several generally useful behaviors into a simple routine: - committing to a single task for a bounded time, - creating frequent checkpoints, - inserting recovery periods, - and reducing re-entry friction through planning. Its effectiveness comes less from the exact minutes and more from (a) how well the interval length matches your task, and (b) whether your breaks and closures minimize switching costs and maximize recovery. If you treat it as an adjustable protocol—timeboxing + planned breaks + closure—then skepticism and evidence-seeking fit naturally: you can test it, measure outcomes, and keep what works.
I started using Anki but I'm drowning in reviews. Am I making cards wrong?

Usually it’s a mix of card design, settings, and daily workflow. “Drowning” often comes from (1) too many new cards too fast, (2) cards that are too broad or ambiguous, (3) using “Hard/Again” in a way that snowballs, and/or (4) not controlling leeches and lapses. To diagnose quickly: 1) How many reviews/day are you seeing and how many new cards/day are you adding? 2) Are your cards mostly basic Q→A, cloze deletions, or something else? 3) Do you feel like you’re failing because you “don’t know it,” or because the card is confusing/too big? 4) What are your current deck options for New cards/day, Steps (in minutes), Graduating interval, and Easy interval?

Reviews are like 250-400 a day and climbing. New cards I set to 30/day because I wanted to keep momentum. Cards are mostly cloze deletions because it felt efficient. Failing is a mix: sometimes I genuinely don’t remember, but a lot of times I’m like “what is this even asking.” I haven’t touched most settings; I think steps are 1 10? Graduating interval maybe 1 day. Easy maybe 4 days. I just hit Again a lot.

That pattern is extremely common: 30 new/day + cloze-heavy + ambiguous prompts = review explosion. The good news is you can fix it without abandoning spaced repetition. Here’s a structured plan: A) Stop the bleeding (next 7–14 days) 1) Reduce New cards/day to 0–5 temporarily. You need to let the backlog stabilize. You can still “learn,” but don’t introduce a flood into an already flooded system. 2) Add a daily time cap mindset: reviews first, then new. If you can’t finish reviews, add zero new that day. 3) Don’t be afraid to “Suspend” cards that are unclear. If you read a card and think “what is this even asking,” suspend it and fix later. Unclear cards create repeated failures, which create more reviews. B) Fix card design (the main lever) Cloze deletions are not automatically efficient; they can be efficient when each cloze tests one crisp fact and the surrounding context is stable. When clozes get long or have multiple possible answers, your brain can’t form a reliable retrieval cue. A good card is: - One question, one answer. - A stable prompt that’s unambiguous. - Tests recall, not recognition (but doesn’t require writing an essay). - Minimal and atomic: small enough to be answered in ~5–15 seconds. C) Adjust learning steps so “Again” doesn’t punish you excessively Default steps 1 10 can be okay, but if you’re failing lots of cards, you end up looping them constantly in a session, exhausting yourself. Sometimes adding a longer step (e.g., 1 10 1440) helps; sometimes simplifying helps. We’ll tune after we see your failure rate. Before tweaking settings too much, I’d focus on: reducing new cards, cleaning card design, and using suspend aggressively for unclear items. If you can, describe one or two representative cloze cards that feel confusing (just paste the text with the clozes). I’ll show how to rewrite them into better prompts.

Here’s a typical one: “The main components of a good system are {{c1::planning}}, {{c2::execution}}, {{c3::review}}, and {{c4::feedback}}.” Another: “When prioritizing, use the {{c1::Eisenhower matrix}} to separate {{c2::urgent}} from {{c3::important}} tasks.” I have a lot like that. They seem straightforward but I still blank.

These are “list clozes” and “definition clozes.” They feel straightforward when you read them, but during recall they can be slippery because: 1) Multiple blanks in one sentence can create interference. You might remember 2 of 4 but not the exact phrasing/order. 2) The prompt is not a strong retrieval cue. “Main components of a good system” could be many frameworks. 3) They often test “verbatim recall” of a list rather than a concept you can apply. How to improve: Option 1: One blank per card, but add a strong cue Instead of four clozes, split into separate cards with a cue that anchors the framework: - Q: “In the ‘planning → execution → review → feedback’ loop, what comes after planning?” A: execution - Q: “In the same loop, what stage turns outcomes into adjustments?” A: feedback - Q: “Name the four stages of the planning loop (in order).” A: planning, execution, review, feedback (This one is optional and should be used sparingly.) Option 2: Convert to “why/how” micro-cards You’ll remember better if each card ties the term to its role. - Q: “What is the purpose of the ‘review’ stage in the planning loop?” A: evaluate what happened vs. plan; identify gaps and lessons - Q: “What does ‘feedback’ mean in the loop?” A: incorporate lessons into the next plan; adjust system inputs/constraints For the Eisenhower matrix card, “separate urgent from important” is too generic. Better: - Q: “In the Eisenhower matrix, what defines ‘urgent’?” A: time-sensitive, requires immediate attention - Q: “In the Eisenhower matrix, what defines ‘important’?” A: contributes to long-term goals/values; high impact - Q: “Which quadrant should you schedule?” A: important but not urgent - Q: “Which quadrant should you delegate (when possible)?” A: urgent but not important You can still use cloze, but keep it to one cloze per note or use multiple clozes only when they are truly independent. Question: when you blank, is it because you can’t recall the term at all, or because you can’t recall the exact wording you used in the cloze?
It’s often exact wording. Like I know the idea but can’t remember the word I used. And then I hit Again and feel punished. Also I think I made too many cards that are basically “name the thing.”

That’s a key insight: you’re learning labels rather than building retrieval routes. Labels matter, but if most cards are “name the thing,” you end up with brittle recall. Two tactics: 1) Accept synonyms when appropriate If the concept is what matters, don’t force one exact word unless precision is necessary. In Anki, you can: - Put acceptable variants on the answer side (e.g., “time-sensitive / immediate / deadline-driven”). - Or phrase the question so multiple correct phrasings are okay: “Define urgent (time aspect).” 2) Shift from “name” to “discriminate” Better than “What is X?” is “What’s the difference between X and Y?” or “Given a scenario, which quadrant is it?” Even without specifying any real-world context, you can create mini decision prompts: - Q: “If something has a deadline soon but low impact, is it urgent, important, both, or neither?” A: urgent, not important - Q: “If something has high impact but no deadline pressure, which category?” A: important, not urgent These force you to retrieve the rule, not just the label. About feeling punished: hitting Again is correct if you truly couldn’t retrieve. The “punishment” comes from too many such cards and too high new intake. Once you reduce new cards and improve prompts, Again becomes less frequent. Let’s set a practical stabilization target: aim for ~100–150 reviews/day for a while. To get there, we’ll adjust new cards and possibly intervals. Do you know your retention rate (Anki stats: “Answer Buttons” or “True retention”)? If not, tell me roughly: are you failing 30% of reviews? 10%? 50%?
It feels like I fail a lot. Maybe 35-40%? Especially on days when I’m tired. I didn’t even know about true retention. I just see the red Again button constantly.

35–40% failure is exhausting and will blow up workload. A sustainable zone for many people is closer to ~80–90% review success (so 10–20% Again), depending on goals and time. High failure usually means one or more of: - Cards too hard/ambiguous - Too many new cards (not enough consolidation) - Reviewing when depleted without adjusting expectations - Learning steps not matching your attention span - Not enough “encoding” before Anki (Anki is for practice, not first exposure) Concrete steps: 1) Immediate change: New cards/day = 0 for 3 days Just clear reviews and mark/suspend problem cards. This gives you breathing room. 2) “Fix as you go” rule When you miss a card: - If you missed because you didn’t learn it yet: mark it and move on, but consider adding a quick note or mnemonic. - If you missed because the prompt is unclear: edit immediately (or suspend). - If you missed because it’s too big: split into 2–4 smaller cards. 3) Add an “encoding pass” before adding new cards Before you make cards, do a short pass where you rewrite the idea in your own words. Then make cards from that rewrite. This reduces “verbatim cloze” traps. 4) Consider adjusting learning steps If you’re using Steps: 1 10, and you keep failing, you’re seeing the same card again and again in a short window, which can feel like grinding. You can try: - Steps: 10 1440 (meaning: first learning repetition at 10 min, then next day) or - Steps: 5 30 1440 (a bit more reinforcement before next day) This reduces immediate loops and can improve morale, but it may increase short-term forgetting. The bigger win is card quality + fewer new. If you want, I can propose a “baseline configuration” that’s conservative and stable. Are you using default scheduler (v3) and default ease settings? Any add-ons?

Default scheduler, no add-ons. I’m scared of messing with settings because I heard you can ruin your deck. Also I don’t know how to “encode” without spending forever. I thought Anki was supposed to be the learning.

You can change settings safely if you do it gradually and avoid extreme values. You won’t “ruin” your deck; at worst you create more or fewer reviews, and you can correct it. On encoding: Anki is best as retrieval practice after some initial comprehension. If you add cards straight from a source without processing, you’re asking Anki to do both understanding and memorization. That’s when you get the “I recognize it but can’t recall” cycle. A fast encoding workflow (5–15 minutes, not forever): 1) Read a small chunk. 2) Close it and write 3–5 bullet points from memory in your own words. 3) Compare to the original, correct errors. 4) Turn each bullet into 1–3 small cards. This is “generation,” which strengthens memory before Anki even starts. Safe baseline settings (conservative): - New cards/day: 5–10 (after backlog stabilizes) - Maximum reviews/day: leave high (9999) so you don’t create hidden debt, but use a time cap personally - Steps (in minutes): 10 1440 (or 5 30 1440) - Graduating interval: 3 days (instead of 1) if you’re doing a next-day step; otherwise keep 1 - Easy interval: 7 days - Lapses steps: 10 1440 (or 10 1440 4320 if you want more) - Leech threshold: 6–8; action: Tag Only or Suspend (I prefer Suspend for sanity) But I’d like to tailor: do you review once per day or multiple sessions? And do you typically do Anki at a consistent time?
Once per day, usually at night when I’m already tired. Sometimes I skip and then it’s worse. I don’t have a consistent time. And the idea of suspending feels like cheating.

Night + tired + inconsistent time will inflate failures. Spaced repetition is sensitive to state: when you’re depleted, retrieval is harder, you press Again more, and the backlog grows. Three practical adjustments: 1) Move reviews earlier if possible Even 20 minutes earlier in the day can change outcomes. If you can’t, split: 10 minutes earlier + the rest at night. 2) “Minimum viable Anki” rule On bad days, do a small non-zero amount (e.g., 50 reviews), not zero. This prevents backlog spikes. You can set a custom study session or just stop after 50. 3) Suspending is not cheating; it’s quality control If a card is unclear or not worth the time, suspending is like removing a broken item from a conveyor belt. You can tag it “fixme” and revisit weekly. Let’s build you a weekly maintenance routine: - Daily: reviews; fix obvious broken cards on the spot - Weekly (30–45 min): open “fixme” tag, edit/split/delete, unsuspend - Monthly: check stats, adjust new cards up/down Now, about your cloze habit: cloze is fine, but you need to design cloze prompts that force recall of meaning, not just fill-in-the-blank. Would you be willing to try a rule: “No more than 1 cloze per sentence unless each blank could be answered independently”?
Yes. But I’m worried that splitting cards means I’ll have even more cards and more reviews. Isn’t that worse?

Counterintuitively, splitting usually reduces total time and reviews because each card becomes easier and has a higher success rate. One hard card can generate many repeated failures (and emotional fatigue). Four easy cards can be answered quickly with fewer lapses. Think in terms of “seconds per successful recall,” not “number of cards.” A big ambiguous card might take 30 seconds and still fail; a small atomic card might take 6 seconds and pass. Also, splitting lets Anki schedule each piece appropriately. If you half-know a big card, the whole thing gets punished. With atomic cards, the parts you know can move out to longer intervals, and only the weak parts stay frequent. However, don’t split into trivia fragments that lose meaning. The sweet spot is “atomic but meaningful.” A practical heuristic: - If you can’t answer within 10–15 seconds, the card is too big or unclear. - If you can answer in 2 seconds without thinking, it might be too easy or recognition-based (unless it’s foundational). Do you use the “Again/Hard/Good/Easy” buttons consistently? Many people overuse Hard.
I mostly use Again if I miss, Good if I get it, and sometimes Hard when I hesitate. I almost never use Easy because I’m afraid it’ll disappear and I’ll forget.

That’s reasonable. Two tweaks: 1) Use Easy strategically Easy isn’t “banish forever”; it just increases interval more. If a card is genuinely trivial for you, pressing Easy reduces workload without harming retention much. If you never press Easy, you keep too many cards at shorter intervals, increasing daily load. 2) Redefine “hesitate” Hesitation can mean two different things: - Productive effort: you retrieved after searching memory. That’s good; press Good. - Confusion/guessing: you weren’t sure and got lucky. That’s Hard or Again depending on confidence. A simple rule: - If you could explain why the answer is correct: Good. - If you guessed: Hard/Again. Also, fear of forgetting is normal, but Anki’s algorithm is designed to bring it back before you completely lose it—assuming your cards are clear. Let’s talk about “mature cards” and lapses. Are you frequently failing cards that you previously knew well?
Yes! That’s what makes me panic. I’ll see something I thought I knew and then my mind is blank. Then it comes back after I press Again and see the answer and I’m like “I knew that.”

That experience is common and not necessarily a sign of failure. Memory retrieval is probabilistic and state-dependent. Occasional lapses are expected. But frequent mature lapses can come from: - Interference: too many similar cards with similar prompts - Context-dependent cues: the card’s wording doesn’t match how you think about it - Overlapping clozes (“main components of a good system…” vs “key stages of a good process…”) - Reviewing when exhausted Solutions: 1) Differentiate similar cards If you have multiple frameworks that sound alike, add distinct cues in the question. Example: include the name of the framework or a unique keyword in the prompt. 2) Add “disambiguation” to the front Instead of: “The main components of a good system are ___” Use: “In the [specific framework name], the four stages are ___.” 3) Use images or structure Even a simple formatting change can create a stronger cue: headings, bullets, consistent ordering. 4) Add “why” on the back On the answer side, add a 1-line explanation. That helps re-encode on review. Also consider reviewing earlier in the day to reduce state-related lapses. Do you have many cards that are basically the same idea rephrased?
Probably. I made cards from different notes and didn’t deduplicate. So I’ll have “urgent vs important” in like five different cloze sentences. I thought repetition was good.

Repetition is good when it’s spaced and varied meaningfully, but duplicate cards can create two problems: - Extra workload without extra learning - Interference because prompts compete Better approach: one strong “canonical” card + a couple of application cards, not five near-identical definitions. Dedup strategy: 1) Search for repeated keywords (like “urgent” “important”) and browse those notes. 2) Keep the clearest 1–2 cards; suspend or delete the rest. 3) If you want variety, make them different in type: definition, discrimination, and application. Example set: - Definition card: “Define urgent.” - Definition card: “Define important.” - Discrimination card: “High impact, no deadline pressure: urgent/important?” - Action card: “Important-not-urgent tasks: what’s the recommended handling?” (schedule) That’s four cards that cover the concept better than ten clozes. Would you be okay doing a one-time “deck cleanup” session? Even 60–90 minutes can drastically reduce stress.
I think so, but I’m overwhelmed by the idea of opening the browser and seeing thousands of cards. I don’t know what to delete. And I’m scared I’ll delete something I need.

Then don’t delete at first—suspend. Suspending is reversible and psychologically safer. A low-overwhelm cleanup protocol: 1) Create a tag: “cleanup_candidate” and “fixme.” 2) In the browser, pick ONE narrow keyword you know is duplicated. 3) Sort by “Due” or “Interval” to see mature cards first. 4) For each cluster: - Keep the best one active (clear, atomic, good cue). - Tag the rest as cleanup_candidate and suspend them. 5) After a week, if you didn’t miss them, you can delete or keep suspended. Also: you don’t need to clean everything. Even fixing the worst 10% often yields most of the benefit. Let’s also address your new card intake. 30/day is high unless you have lots of time and high-quality cards. A sustainable pace for many people is 5–15/day. If your reviews are 300/day now, set new cards to 0–5 until reviews fall under ~150/day for a week. Then slowly increase by 2/day. Do you know approximately how many minutes per day you can realistically spend on Anki?
Realistically maybe 30-45 minutes. But lately it’s been like 90 minutes and I still don’t finish. Then I feel guilty and avoid it.

With 30–45 minutes/day, you need to keep average review volume manageable. Many people average ~6–12 seconds per review when things are healthy. At 10 seconds/review, 200 reviews is ~33 minutes. If your cards are taking longer because they’re confusing, 200 reviews becomes 60+ minutes. So your goal should be: - Reduce daily reviews to ~150–250 depending on speed - Improve card clarity so average time/review drops - Keep new cards low until stable Behavioral fix for avoidance: - Set a timer for 20 minutes. Do reviews. Stop when timer ends. - Do a second 10–20 minute session later if you can. This prevents “infinite Anki sessions” that feel punishing. Now, let’s refine card types. You said you make many “name the thing” cards. A balanced deck often has: - 30–40% definitions/labels - 30–40% discrimination (compare, choose, classify) - 20–30% procedures/checklists (steps) - Some “example generation” (produce an example) if applicable Even without specifying a real-world context, you can make abstract mini prompts. Do you have any cards that are procedural, like “steps to do X”?
Yes, lots. Like “Steps for weekly review: collect, process, organize, review, do.” I clozed each step. Those are some of the worst. I can never remember the exact sequence.

Sequences are notoriously hard as pure recall lists. Better options: 1) Chunk the sequence Instead of five separate clozes, create a structured cue: - Q: “Weekly review has five stages: C-P-O-R-D. Expand the acronym.” A: collect, process, organize, review, do Then add smaller cards: - Q: “In weekly review, what happens in ‘collect’?” A: gather inputs from inboxes, notes, open loops - Q: “In weekly review, what’s the goal of ‘process’?” A: clarify what each item is and next actions This way you remember meaning, not just order. 2) Use “next step” cards - Q: “In weekly review, after ‘organize’ comes what?” A: review - Q: “Before ‘organize’ comes what?” A: process 3) Use recognition sparingly for ordering Sometimes a multiple-choice style is okay for sequences if your goal is operational. But Anki doesn’t do MCQ great; you can simulate it with “Which comes next: A/B/C?” but keep it minimal. Also: if the exact order isn’t crucial, don’t test exact order. Test the components and purpose. Do you actually need perfect order recall, or just to be able to perform the routine?
I mostly want to be able to do it, not recite it. I think I got tricked into memorizing slogans.

Great—then shift your cards to “execution cues” rather than “recitation cues.” Examples: - Q: “During weekly review, what’s the first action to reduce mental load?” A: collect everything into inboxes - Q: “If you find an item with no next action, what do you do?” A: clarify outcome/next action or trash/incubate - Q: “What list should you scan to spot stalled commitments?” A: projects list (or equivalent) These are functional prompts. Another tool: “If-then” cards. - Q: “If an item takes <2 minutes, what’s the rule?” A: do it now - Q: “If an item requires someone else, what’s the next action?” A: delegate and track in waiting-for list This builds retrieval that matches doing. Now, about cloze: you can still use it for “if-then” rules, but keep the prompt strong: “If a task takes less than two minutes, then {{c1::do it immediately}}.” But better might be Basic: Front: “Two-minute rule?” Back: “If it takes <2 minutes, do it now.” How many cards are you adding per day roughly (notes/cards)? Is 30 new cards/day from 30 notes, or from cloze notes that generate multiple cards each?
Oh… it’s from cloze notes that generate multiple cards. So 30 new cards/day might be like 10 notes but each has 3-6 clozes. I didn’t realize that.

That explains the explosion. Cloze notes can multiply fast. If each note yields 5 cards and you add 10 notes/day, you’re effectively creating 50 cards/day even if you “only” introduce 30 new/day. Two fixes: 1) Limit clozes per note (1–2 max, ideally). 2) Set new cards/day based on cards, not notes (Anki uses cards). If your backlog is huge, start with 5 new cards/day total. Also consider “bury related new cards” and “bury related reviews” (options). That prevents seeing siblings on the same day, which can cause false familiarity and later forgetting. In v3 scheduler, there are settings for burying siblings; ensure they’re on. Do you see multiple cloze deletions from the same sentence appearing back-to-back?
Yes, all the time. I answer one and then the next is basically the same sentence with a different blank. It feels like cheating and also boring.

Turn on sibling burying (both for new and reviews). That alone improves efficiency and reduces the “illusion of mastery.” Then restructure those notes: - One cloze per note, or - Convert to Basic cards with varied prompts A quick refactor method: - For each multi-cloze sentence, decide the single most important blank. Keep that as the only cloze. - Move other items to separate notes with different prompts or to the back as supportive info. Example: Original: “Components: planning, execution, review, feedback.” Keep one cloze: “The loop is: planning → execution → review → {{c1::feedback}}.” And on the back include the full list. But again, better: a “what comes next” card. Now let’s deal with your current backlog. Are you behind on due reviews (like, a number that says 1000 due)?
Yes. It says like 1200 due sometimes. I panic and close it.

You need an “Anki debt” plan. Option A (recommended): Pay it down gradually - Set New cards/day = 0. - Do a fixed number of reviews/day above the daily due, like +50/day. But Anki doesn’t show “daily due” easily; instead, set a daily target: e.g., 250 reviews/day until due count drops. Option B: One-time sprint - Take 2–3 days and do 500–800 reviews/day. This is brutal and often leads to burnout. Option C: Reschedule (not ideal, but sometimes necessary) - Use “Set Due Date” to spread overdue cards across future days. This reduces immediate panic but doesn’t fix card quality. Given you’re already overwhelmed, I’d do Option A with a hard time cap. Example: - Two sessions/day, 20 minutes each. - No new cards. - Suspend any card that takes >20 seconds or feels unclear. Within a week, the due count often drops significantly because you stop generating new debt. Do you want a very concrete 7-day schedule you can follow?
Yes, please. I need something I can just do without thinking.

7-day stabilization schedule (minimal thinking): Day 1: - Set New cards/day = 0. - Turn on “bury siblings” (new + reviews). - Do 40 minutes of reviews total (split if needed). - Every time you feel “what is this asking?” → Suspend + tag “fixme”. Day 2: - New = 0. - 40 minutes reviews. - If you fail a card twice in the same day → Suspend + tag fixme (don’t grind). Day 3: - New = 0. - 40 minutes reviews. - After reviews, 10 minutes: open browser → tag:fixme → pick 10 cards → edit for clarity or split; unsuspend if fixed. Day 4: - New = 0. - 45 minutes reviews. - Continue suspend-on-confusion. Day 5: - New = 0. - 45 minutes reviews. - 15 minutes fixme editing. Day 6: - New = 0. - 45 minutes reviews. Day 7: - New = 0. - 30 minutes reviews. - 30 minutes cleanup: find one duplicated concept cluster, suspend duplicates. After Day 7: - If due count is trending down and your failure rate is improving, set New = 5/day. - If still drowning, keep New = 0 for another week. Rules to reduce emotional load: - You are allowed to stop when the timer ends. - You are allowed to suspend. - You are not allowed to add new cards until reviews feel “contained.” Now: editing cards. You said cloze lists are a problem. Do you want a template for converting list clozes into better card types?
Yes. I have so many list clozes. I need a recipe.

Recipe for list-heavy material: Step 1: Decide the purpose of the list - Is it a checklist you must perform? - Is it categories for classification? - Is it just “things to remember exist”? Step 2: Choose one of these card patterns Pattern A: “Name the list” (use sparingly) Front: “What are the 4 parts of X (in order)?” Back: “A, B, C, D.” Use only if order matters. Pattern B: “Identify missing element” Front: “X consists of A, B, C, and ____.” Back: “D.” This is easier than full recall but still retrieval. Pattern C: “Role of each element” (best for understanding) Make one card per element: Front: “In X, what does B do / mean?” Back: “B = … (function).” Pattern D: “Discriminate elements” Front: “Which element of X handles [function]?” Back: “B.” Pattern E: “When to use” Front: “If you see [cue], which element of X applies?” Back: “B.” Step 3: Add a cue that pins the framework Always include the framework name or a distinctive phrase on the front. Otherwise lists blend. Step 4: Keep answer format consistent If you want exact terms, keep them stable. If synonyms are okay, list them. Also: if you have a list of 10 items, consider whether it should be memorized at all. Sometimes it’s better as a reference note, not Anki. How often do you find yourself memorizing long lists that you never actually use?
Too often. I think I got addicted to turning everything into cards. It felt productive. Now it’s crushing me.

That’s the classic trap: “card creation feels like progress.” Real progress is being able to retrieve what matters with low friction. A practical filter before making a card: 1) Will I need to recall this from memory, quickly, without looking it up? 2) Is forgetting it costly? 3) Is it foundational (supports many other ideas)? If “no,” consider not ankifying it. Put it in a searchable note instead. Another filter: “one-week test.” If you wouldn’t care to recall it in one week, don’t make it a card. Also consider a “parking lot” deck/tag: - Tag: “reference_only” for items you might want later but not for daily review. Now, about your workflow: how do you create cards? Directly in Anki while reading/notes, or do you draft elsewhere then import?
Directly in Anki. I open Add, paste a sentence, make clozes, hit Add, repeat. It’s fast. But now I see it’s not good.

Direct-in-Anki is fine, but add a tiny friction step to improve quality: The “10-second design check” before hitting Add: - Can this be answered in one short phrase? - Is the question unambiguous? - Is there only one thing being tested? - Would I understand what I meant 6 months from now? If any answer is “no,” revise before adding. Also: consider switching some cloze notes to Basic. Cloze encourages copying sentences; Basic encourages you to craft a prompt. You can keep cloze for: - Short definitions - Equations/formulas (if applicable) - Very stable phrasing where fill-in is appropriate But for frameworks and processes, Basic is often better. Do you know how to use “Cloze note type” vs “Basic” note type? If not, I can outline a simple setup.
I know the basics but I default to cloze because it’s one click. Basic feels like more work.

Make Basic easier by creating a keyboard-driven workflow: - Add window: choose Basic - Front: write question - Back: write answer + 1-line explanation - Use a consistent prefix like “Define:” “Compare:” “If:” “Purpose:” to speed creation You can also create a custom note type “Basic+Context” with fields: - Prompt - Answer - Extra/context Then show Extra on the back in smaller text. This reduces your need to cram context into the prompt. If you want to keep cloze convenience but reduce harm: - Restrict yourself to one cloze per note - Rewrite the sentence into a question-like statement Example: “Urgent means {{c1::time-sensitive}}” is okay. But “Use the matrix to separate urgent from important” is vague. Now let’s address the emotional cycle: panic → avoid → backlog → more panic. The fix is a “no drama” daily ritual. Would you be willing to set a fixed daily trigger, like “after breakfast do 15 minutes”?
I can try. I’m not a morning person but I can do something earlier than midnight. I just hate the feeling of endless reviews.

Then make it finite: - Set a timer for 15 minutes at a consistent time. - Stop when it rings, even if reviews remain. - Optional: second 15 minutes later in the day. Consistency beats hero sessions. Also, you can use Anki’s “Deck options → Display order” to reduce stress: - Review order: “Due” is fine; avoid random if it increases context switching - New card order: “Show new cards in order added” can help coherence But the biggest stress reducer is reducing new cards and suspending junk. Do you ever use “Again” when you were close but not exact? That can be too strict depending on your goal.
Yes. If I can’t say it exactly, I hit Again. It feels like failing otherwise.

That’s a perfectionism setting, not a memory setting. Decide what “correct” means for each card: - If the card is about a precise label you truly need exact: be strict. - If the card is about a concept: accept “close enough” and press Good, but maybe add the exact term on the back so you reinforce it. You can also add grading guidance in the card itself: On the back: “Grade: Good if you recalled the idea; Again if you couldn’t classify it.” This reduces internal debate. Now, one technical question: do you have separate decks, or everything in one big deck?
Multiple decks. But I often do them all in one session because I want to clear everything.

Multiple decks are fine, but they can fragment your attention. Consider: - Use a parent deck and study from the parent so Anki interleaves reviews (helps discrimination) - Or keep separate but prioritize: do the most important deck first when fresh Since you’re overwhelmed, I’d prioritize the deck that contains the most foundational cards and suspend low-value decks temporarily. Also, check if you have different option groups per deck. Sometimes one deck has aggressive new cards settings and causes most of the pain. Do you know if all decks share the same options group?
I think they share, but I’m not sure. I might have accidentally made different option groups.

It’s worth checking. In Decks → gear icon → Options. See the name of the options group. Standardize them for now to avoid surprises. If you find one deck with New cards/day = 30 while another is 10, that can explain why workload spikes. Another technical factor: “Maximum interval.” If it’s set very low, mature cards never go away and you get constant reviews. Default is usually 36500 days. If you set it to 365 or something, that increases workload. Worth verifying. Also check “Lapse” settings: if a lapse sends a mature card back to very short intervals too often, it increases load. But again, card quality first. What’s the main thing you want from Anki: long-term retention with low stress, or rapid short-term memorization?
Long-term retention with low stress. I want it to be sustainable, not a second job.

Then your design principles are: - Fewer new cards - Higher card quality - Acceptable retention target (not perfection) - Regular review habit A sustainable retention target might be 85–90%. Chasing 95–98% can double workload. If you want, you can eventually use FSRS (a modern scheduling algorithm) for more efficient intervals, but it’s optional. For now, you can get 80% of the benefit with workflow changes. Let’s focus on your immediate pain: 1200 due. If you follow the 7-day plan, you’ll likely feel relief. But to make sure, we should decide your daily review target. With 40 minutes/day, if your average is currently 15 seconds/review (because of confusion), you can do ~160 reviews. If you improve to 10 seconds, ~240 reviews. So set a daily target of 200 reviews for now. Doable, not crushing. Keep New = 0. Does that feel realistic, or still too much?
200 feels scary but maybe possible with a timer. I just worry I’ll still have due piling up.

If due continues piling up at 200/day, that means your deck is generating more than 200 due per day, which can happen if: - You have a large backlog already due - Your learning steps create lots of same-day repeats - You’re failing many cards (Again creates extra reps) As your failure rate drops (from editing/suspending) and backlog shrinks, daily due will fall. The first few days are the hardest. Also, don’t ignore the power of suspending: if 20% of your deck is low-quality and causing 80% of failures, suspending those will immediately reduce workload. A quick triage rule: If a card has lapsed multiple times (leechy), suspend it. You can rebuild it later in a better form. Do you currently have leech handling turned on? (It tags “leech” after a certain number of lapses.)
I saw leech tags but I never looked at them. I didn’t know what they meant.

Leeches are cards that keep failing—often because they’re poorly written or too similar to others. They’re review vampires. Action plan for leeches: - In browser: search tag:leech - For each: - If it’s genuinely important: rewrite into simpler/clearer cards (often 2–3 cards) - If it’s not important: suspend or delete - Clear the leech tag after fixing so you can track future issues Set leech threshold to 6–8 lapses. And set action to “Suspend” if you want your workload protected. This single step often reduces daily misery. Now, about cloze siblings: you said you see them back-to-back. Turn on burying, then consider “Cloze overlapper” add-on later if you want, but not necessary. Any other pain point? For example, do you feel like reviews are boring, or that you can’t stay focused?
Both. I get bored, then I rush, then I fail more. Also I keep changing the wording of cards and then I don’t recognize them later.

Boredom → rushing → failures is a real feedback loop. Two ways to break it: 1) Make cards more engaging via active prompts “Explain in one sentence” cards can be more engaging than fill-in-the-blank. But keep them short. 2) Use micro-breaks Every 25 reviews, look away for 10 seconds. It sounds silly, but it resets attention. About changing wording: editing is good, but consistency matters. If you constantly rewrite prompts, you remove the stable cue your brain is learning. Instead: - Stabilize the front side (prompt) once it’s clear - Add clarifications on the back side rather than rewriting the front repeatedly - If you do rewrite the front, do it to make it more specific, not just “different” Also avoid “clever” prompts that you won’t understand later. Use plain language. A technique: add a “Context” field that’s shown on the back only. Keep the prompt minimal and consistent. Do you want an example of turning a cloze sentence into a stable Basic card with context?
Yes, that would help.

Example transformation: Original cloze: “When prioritizing, use the Eisenhower matrix to separate urgent from important tasks.” Problems: vague, tests label, doesn’t force you to recall the rule. Better Basic card: Front: “Eisenhower matrix: what’s the key distinction between urgent and important?” Back: “Urgent = time-sensitive / demands immediate attention. Important = high-impact / aligned with longer-term outcomes. Context: Use to decide do/schedule/delegate/eliminate.” Then add a separate card: Front: “Eisenhower matrix: what do you do with ‘important but not urgent’ items?” Back: “Schedule them.” And one discrimination card: Front: “Time-sensitive but low impact: urgent/important?” Back: “Urgent, not important.” This set produces stronger retrieval routes than the original sentence. Now, card count: you might worry this is more cards. But these cards will be easier, pass more, and take less time overall. If you adopt this style, your new-card creation rate might slow slightly, but your review load will drop and retention will improve. Do you currently add images, formatting, or just plain text?
Mostly plain text. Sometimes bold. I don’t use images. I also sometimes paste huge paragraphs and then cloze random phrases.

Huge paragraphs are almost always a mistake in Anki. They create ambiguous prompts and slow reviews. A hard rule: no paragraphs on the front. If you need context, put it on the back or in an Extra field. If you have pasted paragraphs, triage: - If the card takes >20 seconds: suspend - Extract 1–3 atomic facts or rules and make separate cards - Keep the paragraph in a separate reference note (not a card), or in Extra on the back Formatting tip: use bullet points on the back, not the front. The front should be a single clear question. Also, consider “one-screen rule”: the back should ideally fit on one screen without scrolling. How many of your cards would you say are “paragraph clozes”?
Too many. Maybe 20-30%? I thought more context would help.

Context helps understanding, but too much context on the front weakens the retrieval cue because your attention diffuses. Put context on the back so it supports re-encoding after you answer. If you want context on the front, use a very short cue phrase, not a paragraph. Example: Front: “Rule for handling low-value time-sensitive items (matrix).” Back: “Delegate or minimize; they’re urgent-not-important.” Now, you mentioned you skip days. Skipping is deadly for workload because reviews accumulate. If you can’t do full reviews, do a tiny session. Even 5 minutes keeps the habit alive. Anki trick: create a filtered deck for “Overdue” and do a small number daily. But filtered decks can complicate things; simplest is just “do something every day.” Would you prefer to commit to a tiny daily minimum like 10 minutes?
Yes. 10 minutes feels doable. But I’m worried 10 minutes won’t be enough to catch up.

Make it two-tier: - Minimum: 10 minutes/day no matter what - Target: 30–45 minutes on most days - Catch-up: one longer session once per week if needed This keeps you from “all or nothing.” Also, consider changing review environment: - Same place, fewer distractions - Full-screen Anki - Disable notifications Now, I want to make sure you understand spaced repetition itself, because that can reduce anxiety. The goal isn’t to never forget; it’s to forget just enough that retrieval is effortful. Effortful retrieval is what strengthens memory. So occasional blanks are part of the process, not a catastrophe. But your current blank rate is too high, indicating poor encoding/card design. Do you feel like you understand how Anki schedules cards (intervals, ease), or is it a black box?
Mostly a black box. I know it shows cards again later, but I don’t get what ease is or why pressing Hard messes things up.

Quick mental model: - Each card has an interval: how long until you see it again. - Anki estimates how “easy” the card is for you using an ease factor. - When you press: - Again: you failed; it goes into relearning steps and interval shrinks - Hard: you succeeded but barely; interval increases less and ease may decrease - Good: normal success; interval increases as expected - Easy: strong success; interval increases more and ease may increase If you press Hard frequently, ease can drift down, making future intervals shorter, increasing workload. That’s why people warn about “Hard” overuse. It’s not evil, just should be used when you truly barely got it. If you almost never use Easy, your deck can also become “sticky” with shorter intervals. But again: settings and buttons matter less than card quality and new card rate. If you want to avoid ease drift entirely, FSRS can help, but it’s optional. For now, just use Hard sparingly and Easy when truly easy. Do you find yourself pressing Hard because you want to be cautious?
Yes. I’m cautious. If I hesitate at all, I press Hard. I’m afraid of forgetting later.

Try redefining “Hard” to mean “I got it, but I’m not confident I could get it tomorrow.” Not just “I hesitated.” Most good retrieval involves some hesitation. If you retrieved correctly after a few seconds and you understand it, press Good. You can also use a speed threshold: - Answered correctly within 10 seconds: Good - Took 10–20 seconds with uncertainty: Hard - Wrong or blank: Again This makes it consistent. Now, let’s talk about your creation pipeline going forward. Once you stabilize, you’ll want to add new material again without reigniting the fire. A sustainable pipeline: 1) Collect notes in a “to_ankify” list (not immediately into Anki). 2) Once or twice per week, convert a small batch into high-quality cards. 3) Limit to 5–10 new cards/day. This prevents impulsive card creation. Would batching card creation feel better than doing it daily?
Probably. Daily adding makes me feel like I’m keeping up, but it also adds pressure. Batching might help.

Then set a weekly “card writing” block, like 45 minutes on one day. During the week, just do reviews and jot ideas to ankify later. When writing cards in batch: - Keep a strict cap: e.g., create 30–50 cards total, not unlimited. - Focus on high-yield concepts. And always preview: after creating 10 cards, immediately review them (Preview mode) to see if they’re answerable. Another technique: “One source, one framework note.” Instead of making many cards from many places, consolidate into one clean note and then card from that. This reduces duplicates. How do you feel about using tags to manage workflow (to_ankify, fixme, reference)?
I’ve never used tags systematically. It sounds helpful but also like more maintenance.

Keep it minimal: - fixme: unclear cards you suspended - to_ankify: notes you want to convert later - reference: stuff you don’t want in daily reviews That’s it. Three tags. Maintenance is light if you do it in small weekly sessions. Now, I’d like to address a subtle issue: “cloze deletion encourages copying.” To counter that, use “closed-book recall” before making the card: write the idea from memory, then create the card from your own phrasing. This reduces dependence on exact wording. You mentioned you blank on exact wording. This will help because the wording becomes yours and more memorable. Do you have time to try this for just one chunk and see how it feels?
Yes. But I’m worried it’ll slow me down too much.

It will slow creation slightly but speed reviews massively. Think of it as shifting time from endless reviews to higher-quality encoding. A compromise: do it only for complex ideas. For simple definitions, you can still do quick cards. Also, you can use a “progressive summarization” approach: - Level 1: copy the sentence - Level 2: highlight key phrase - Level 3: rewrite in your words Only ankify at level 3 for anything that’s not trivial. Now, do you want to talk about specific settings changes now, or focus purely on workflow and card design for a week?
I want minimal settings changes. I’m scared. But I do want to turn on burying siblings. And maybe change steps if it helps.

Minimal safe settings changes: 1) Turn on bury siblings (new + reviews). 2) Set New cards/day = 0 for one week. 3) Optionally adjust learning steps to reduce grind: - Steps: 10 1440 - Graduating interval: 3 days - Easy interval: 7 days These are conservative and won’t “break” anything. If you’d rather not touch steps yet, skip #3 and just do #1 and #2. You’ll still see improvement from suspending and better cards. One more setting: “New cards ignore review limit” should be off (so you don’t add new when you already have many reviews). But you’re setting new to 0 anyway. Also, keep “Maximum reviews/day” high; don’t artificially cap it in settings because that hides debt. Do you know how to find bury sibling options in your version? If not, I can describe the clicks.
Please describe it. I get lost in the options.

In Anki desktop: 1) Go to Decks. 2) Click the gear icon next to the deck (or parent deck) → Options. 3) In the options window, look for a section like “Burying” or “Display order.” 4) Enable: - “Bury new siblings” - “Bury review siblings” (Some versions say “Bury related new cards until the next day” and same for reviews.) If using a parent deck, set it on the parent options group so it applies. After changing, it affects future scheduling; it won’t retroactively reorder already-buried cards, but it will help immediately. Now: when you suspend cards, do you know the shortcut? In browser or review screen, “Suspend” is usually Ctrl+J (Windows) / Cmd+J (Mac), but check your mapping. Would you use shortcuts, or prefer clicking?
Shortcuts, definitely. Anything to make it faster.

Essential shortcuts: - Space / Enter: show answer / next - 1/2/3/4: Again/Hard/Good/Easy - Ctrl/Cmd+J: suspend/unsuspend - E: edit current note - R: replay audio (if any) - B: browse current note Using E to edit on the fly is powerful: if a card is unclear, press E, fix wording, save, then answer. But avoid endless tinkering mid-session. If it needs major surgery, suspend and tag fixme. Now, you said you have 1200 due sometimes. After you turn new to 0, your only job is to chip away. Don’t look at the number too much; focus on timer. Would you like a “review triage decision tree” for what to do when you miss a card?
Yes. I freeze when I miss. I either keep failing it or I get mad.

Missed-card decision tree (fast): When you see the answer: 1) Was the prompt unclear/ambiguous? - Yes → Suspend + tag fixme (don’t keep reviewing it) - No → go to 2 2) Was the content not actually learned/understood? - Yes → Add a short explanation on the back (1–2 lines) or a mnemonic; press Again; move on - No → go to 3 3) Did you confuse it with a similar item? - Yes → Edit front to include distinguishing cue (e.g., framework name), or add a “compare X vs Y” card; press Again/Hard depending on how wrong - No → go to 4 4) Was it just a temporary blank (you knew it after seeing answer)? - Yes → Press Again if you truly couldn’t retrieve; but consider whether you’re reviewing tired. If it happens often at night, move session earlier. Key: Do not grind the same broken card repeatedly. That’s what causes burnout. Also, if you repeatedly fail because of tiredness, you might do “lighter” reviews at night: only mature cards or only a subset. But again, simplest is earlier review. How do you feel after reading this—more in control, or still overwhelmed?
Slightly more in control. Still overwhelmed because I have so many cards, but at least it’s a plan. I’m also worried that if I suspend a lot, I’m just avoiding learning.

Suspending is temporary triage. You’re not avoiding learning; you’re postponing low-quality prompts until you can learn them efficiently. Think of it like debugging: - You pause the broken process (bad cards) - You fix the underlying issue (card design) - Then you reintroduce it If you keep broken cards active, you’ll “learn” less because you’ll burn out and stop reviewing altogether. If it helps, set a constraint: you can suspend freely, but you must spend 30 minutes per week fixing some of them. That turns suspension into a pipeline, not avoidance. Also, not everything belongs in Anki. A sustainable system includes reference notes, checklists, and external tools. Anki is for things that benefit from repeated retrieval. Now, would you like help designing a “good card checklist” you can keep next to you while creating cards?
Yes. A short checklist would help.

Good-card checklist (printable): 1) Atomic: tests one idea. 2) Clear: prompt has only one plausible answer. 3) Short: answer fits in a phrase/sentence; review <15 seconds. 4) Stable cue: includes framework name or distinctive keyword. 5) Retrieval-based: requires recall, not just recognition. 6) Useful: you’d want to recall it without looking it up. 7) Minimal context on front; extra context on back. 8) Avoid “lists” unless necessary; prefer “role/discriminate/if-then.” If a card fails 2+ times, ask: is it a bad card or a hard idea? If bad card, rewrite; if hard idea, add scaffolding (more cards, better encoding). Now, one more technical angle: your “Again loops.” If you keep hitting Again, you see the card repeatedly in the same session. That can feel like punishment. A psychological trick: - When you fail, take 2 seconds to mentally rephrase the answer and connect it to a cue. Then press Again. This makes the next repetition meaningful. But don’t overdo it; keep pace. Do you want to try changing your steps to reduce same-session repeats, or keep default?
I think I want to reduce same-session repeats. Seeing the same card 5 times makes me furious.

Then set learning steps to reduce immediate repetition. A common pattern for once-per-day reviewers: - Steps: 10 1440 Meaning: first repeat in 10 minutes, then next day. For lapses (relearning), you can use the same: 10 1440. That means if you fail a mature card, you’ll see it again in ~10 minutes, then next day, rather than repeatedly in 1 minute. This reduces grind but means you must trust the process. It’s usually fine if your cards are clear. Also consider “Learning steps in minutes” not being too short. Remove “1” if it causes immediate loops. So you could change from “1 10” to just “10 1440.” After you change, you might initially feel like you’re not “hammering it in,” but retention often improves because you’re less stressed and more consistent. Try it for 2 weeks and check stats.
Okay. I’ll try 10 1440. What about graduating interval and easy interval?

If you have a 1440-minute step (1 day), set Graduating interval to 3 days. That way after you successfully complete learning, it won’t show up again the very next day as a “review” immediately; it gets a bit of breathing room. Easy interval: 7 days is fine. It’s just the first interval if you press Easy on a new card. So: - Steps: 10 1440 - Graduating interval: 3 - Easy interval: 7 Keep everything else default for now. Also, for lapses: - Relearning steps: 10 1440 - Minimum interval: 1 day (default) - Leech threshold: 8, action suspend Now remember: settings won’t fix bad cards. But they will reduce rage-inducing loops.
I’m still stuck on how to actually rewrite my existing cloze cards without spending weeks. I have thousands.

Don’t rewrite everything. Use the 80/20 approach: - Fix the cards that cause the most pain: high lapse count, leeches, long review time, frequent failures. Practical triage: 1) In Stats, find “Again count” or “Difficulty” (depending on version). 2) In Browser, sort by “Lapses” descending. 3) Start with the top 50 worst offenders. Those are likely responsible for a disproportionate amount of your suffering. For each worst card: - If it’s a paragraph cloze: suspend and extract 1–3 atomic cards. - If it’s a list: convert to role/discrimination cards. - If it’s duplicated: keep one, suspend the rest. - If it’s low value: suspend permanently. If you fix 10 cards/day, in a month you’ve fixed 300 of the worst ones. That’s enough to transform the experience. Also, many cards you never fail aren’t a problem even if they’re not perfect. Leave them.
Sorting by lapses sounds doable. I didn’t know you could do that. But I also feel guilty because I already spent time making these cards and now I’m suspending them.

Sunk cost. The time is already spent. The question is: what action now maximizes future learning per minute? Suspending a bad card is not wasted; it’s you protecting future time. Also, you can salvage content: you’re not throwing away the idea, you’re rewriting it into a form your brain can actually retrieve. If guilt is strong, reframe: - The first version was a draft. - You’re iterating toward a better system. Now, one more concept: “desirable difficulty.” Some people interpret that as “make cards hard.” But desirable difficulty means “effortful but solvable.” Your cards are often “effortful but unclear,” which is undesirable. Aim for effortful but clear. Do you want to practice rewriting one of your worst paragraph clozes? You could paste one (without any personal context) and we can refactor it together.

Okay, here’s a bad one: “A productive workflow includes capturing tasks, clarifying next actions, organizing by context, reflecting regularly, and engaging with confidence. The key is to maintain a trusted system.” Then I clozed like “capturing,” “clarifying,” “organizing,” “reflecting,” “engaging,” “trusted system.” It generates six cards.

This is a classic: too many clozes from one vague paragraph. Refactor options: 1) Make one “framework overview” card (optional) Front: “What are the 5 stages of the workflow framework (verbs)?” Back: “Capture, clarify, organize, reflect, engage.” Only if you truly want the list. 2) Better: one card per stage focusing on meaning Front: “Workflow framework: what does ‘capture’ mean?” Back: “Collect inputs/tasks into an external system so you’re not relying on memory.” Front: “Workflow framework: what does ‘clarify’ produce?” Back: “A decision: what is it? is it actionable? if yes, what’s the next action/outcome?” Front: “Workflow framework: what does ‘organize’ involve?” Back: “Put reminders in the right places (lists/calendars) so you can find them when needed.” Front: “Workflow framework: what is ‘reflect’?” Back: “Regularly review lists to keep them current and trusted.” Front: “Workflow framework: what enables ‘engage with confidence’?” Back: “A trusted system + clear next actions; you choose what to do based on current constraints.” 3) “Trusted system” as a concept card Front: “What makes a system ‘trusted’?” Back: “It reliably contains current commitments; you review it regularly; you believe it’s complete.” Notice each prompt is specific and answerable without reciting the paragraph. You can keep the paragraph in Extra on the back as reference, but don’t test every word. If you still want cloze, do one per note: “‘Capture’ means {{c1::collect inputs into an external system}}.” But Basic is better here. Also, organize by naming: include “Workflow framework” in every front so cues are consistent.
That makes sense. But then how do I prevent myself from making too many cards again? I get carried away.

Put hard limits in your process: - Weekly card creation budget: e.g., 50 cards/week. - Daily new card introduction: 5/day. - “One concept, max 5 cards” rule: if you hit 5, stop and ask if you’re over-encoding. Also use “card types” quota: For each concept/framework: - 1 definition/overview - 2 discrimination/application - 1 “why”/purpose - 1 common confusion That’s 5. It’s plenty. Another guardrail: after making a card, ask “Would I be okay reviewing this for the next year?” If not, don’t add it. Finally: keep a “not worth ankifying” list. When you feel the urge, put it there instead.
I like the budget idea. But I also worry about forgetting stuff if I don’t ankify it.

You won’t remember everything; the goal is to remember what matters. For the rest, build a reliable retrieval system via search and structure (notes, index, tags). Not everything needs spaced repetition. Also, for many ideas, understanding + occasional use is enough. Anki is best for items that you won’t naturally retrieve often but still want accessible. If you over-ankify, you create a second job and eventually abandon it, losing everything. So selective ankifying is actually the best way to retain more over time.
Okay. So for now: new cards to 0, bury siblings, adjust steps, suspend unclear cards, and do 10 minutes minimum daily with a target of 40 minutes. Then weekly fixme cleanup. Right?

Exactly. And one more: stop making multi-cloze list notes. One cloze max or switch to Basic with clear prompts. If you do just those, you’ll likely see: - Reviews stop climbing - Failure rate drops - Sessions feel less punishing After two weeks, check stats: - Review success rate - Average answer time - Reviews/day trend Then decide whether to reintroduce new cards at 5/day.
What if I’m still drowning after two weeks? I’m scared it won’t work.

If after two weeks you’re still drowning, there are additional levers: 1) Suspend aggressively If you suspend 20–30% of lowest-value cards, workload can drop immediately. You can later reintroduce only what you truly need. 2) Reduce retention target indirectly Use Easy more, Hard less, accept “close enough” on conceptual cards. 3) Consider rescheduling overdue cards Use “Set Due Date” to spread 1000 overdue cards across 30 days. This is a pragmatic reset. It’s not ideal, but it’s better than quitting. 4) Use FSRS FSRS can reduce reviews for the same retention target. But it requires a bit of setup and trust. 5) Split decks by priority Only keep high-priority deck active daily; suspend the rest temporarily. But usually, the first plan works if you actually stop adding new and fix/suspend broken cards.
I’m curious about FSRS, but also scared. What is it and would it help me?

FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) is an alternative scheduling algorithm that can be more efficient than Anki’s default ease-factor system. It models your memory parameters and can better predict optimal intervals, often reducing reviews while maintaining retention. Would it help? Potentially, especially if your deck is large and you want fewer reviews. But it doesn’t solve bad cards or too many new cards. It’s like a better engine, but you still need good tires and not overload the car. If you want minimal risk: - Stabilize first (2–4 weeks) - Then consider FSRS If you jump to FSRS while overwhelmed, it can feel like too many moving parts.
Got it. One last thing: when I edit cards, should I reset them or keep their schedule?

Usually keep their schedule unless the meaning changed significantly. Guidelines: - If you only clarified wording or added context: keep schedule. - If you changed what the card is testing (new question/answer): reset (Forget) or set it as new, because the history no longer matches. - If you split one card into several: the new cards should be new (or at least have short intervals) because they’re new prompts. In Anki, you can use “Forget” to reset learning state. Use it sparingly. Also, when you delete duplicates, no need to reset anything; just remove them.
Thanks. I’m going to do Day 1 tonight but earlier than usual. I’m still nervous.

Keep it simple tonight: - New = 0 - Bury siblings on - 20-minute timer (not 2 hours) - Suspend unclear cards without guilt If you finish early and feel okay, do another 10–20 minutes later. If not, stop. Consistency over intensity. Tomorrow, do the minimum again. After 3–4 days, you should feel the reviews becoming less chaotic. If you want, after Day 3 you can tell me: average reviews completed, how many you suspended, and how it felt. Then we can adjust.

I did Day 1. I did 180 reviews in about 45 minutes. It was rough but not as awful because I suspended like 25 cards that were confusing. I turned on bury siblings and it helped a lot. Due count still huge though.

That’s a strong start. Suspending 25 confusing cards in one session is exactly the kind of triage that reduces future pain. For Day 2: - Repeat the same pattern. - If you notice a theme in the suspended cards (lists, paragraphs, duplicates), jot it down. That will guide your fixme session. Also, track two numbers (just roughly): - Again rate: how many times you hit Again out of total reviews - Average time/review (Anki stats or your sense) If you can bring Again rate down even from 40% to 25% within a week, workload will start to stabilize. Did you feel like most fails were from unclear prompts or genuine forgetting?
More unclear prompts than I expected. A lot were like “what does this mean” clozes. Also I found duplicates. I suspended those too.

Perfect. Unclear prompts are the easiest win because fixing them improves retention immediately. For duplicates: choose one “canonical” card and keep it. Make it the best version: - Clear prompt - Short answer - Framework name included Then suspend the rest. Also consider adding a note on the canonical card’s back: “Related: [other term]” so you don’t feel like you’re losing coverage. Day 3 includes 10 minutes of fixme editing. Pick the top 10 most annoying suspended cards and rewrite them into Basic “role/discriminate/if-then” cards. Keep it small. One more trick: when editing, don’t aim for perfection. Aim for “answerable in 10 seconds.”
Day 2 I did 200 reviews in 40 minutes. Again rate still high but a bit lower. I suspended another 15. I noticed many cards start with “The key is to…” which is vague.

Great observation. “The key is to…” is almost always a red flag in prompts because it doesn’t specify which concept you’re retrieving. Rewrite rule: - Replace “The key is to…” with a named concept and a concrete question. Example: Bad: “The key is to {{c1::maintain a trusted system}}.” Better: “What’s the core principle of the workflow framework?” → “Maintain a trusted system.” Even better: “What makes a system trusted?” → criteria. Or: “Why is a trusted system important?” → “Reduces cognitive load; enables confident choice of next actions.” So in your fixme session, search for “key is” and rewrite those. Also, consider banning certain vague stems in your card creation: - “The key is…” - “It is important to…” - “In order to…” These tend to produce fluffy cards. Replace with: - “Define…” - “What is the purpose of…” - “What’s the difference between…” - “If X, what should you do…”

Day 3 I did the reviews and then edited 10 fixme cards. It took longer than I thought because I kept thinking of better ways to phrase. I also realized I don’t know what to do with cards that are basically motivational statements.

Editing can become a rabbit hole. Put a timer on editing too: 15 minutes max. After that, stop. For motivational statements: most do not belong in Anki. They’re not stable facts and they don’t benefit from exact recall. They often become vague prompts that you “know” but can’t recite, causing pointless failures. Options: 1) Delete them. 2) Move them to a “reference” note outside Anki. 3) Convert only the actionable part into an if-then rule. Example: Motivational: “Consistency is more important than intensity.” If you truly want it in Anki, make it actionable: Front: “When tempted to skip because you can’t do a full session, what’s the rule?” Back: “Do a small minimum session; consistency beats intensity.” That’s at least behaviorally specific. But be selective—too many of these become noise.
That helps. I think I’ll delete most of those. I feel weird deleting but also relieved. My due count is still high but I’m not panicking as much. When can I add new cards again?

Add new cards again when two conditions are true for at least 7 consecutive days: 1) You finish your daily reviews within your time budget most days (e.g., 30–45 minutes). 2) Your Again rate is in a tolerable range (say 10–20% for reviews; learning cards can be higher). Then start with New = 5/day. After a week, if stable, increase to 7/day, then 10/day. Never jump back to 30/day. Also, remember: if you have a backlog, adding new cards delays recovery. So wait until due count is no longer psychologically threatening. You can also do “new cards only on weekends” or “new cards 3 days/week” to control load.

I’m on Day 7 now. Reviews are down to around 220/day and I’m finishing in about 45 minutes. Again rate feels like 20-25% now. Due count is still big but smaller. I suspended maybe 120 cards total. Is that too many?

120 suspended cards is not too many if they were confusing/duplicative/low value. It’s a sign you’re actively improving deck quality. 20–25% Again is much better than 40%. You’re trending in the right direction. Next week plan: - Keep New = 0 for one more week if you want faster debt payoff. Or - Start New = 5/day if you feel stable and motivated. Given your due count is still big, I’d personally do one more week of New = 0, then introduce New = 5/day. But if you’re itching to add, 5/day won’t ruin you as long as you keep quality high and stop adding if reviews spike. Also, schedule a weekly 30-minute fixme session to gradually rebuild suspended cards into better ones.
I think I’ll wait one more week. I don’t want to slide back. Can you help me create a “card creation style guide” so when I do start adding again, I don’t repeat the same mistakes?

Style guide (simple and strict): 1) Default to Basic, not Cloze Use Cloze only when: - the sentence is short - only one blank - the prompt is unambiguous 2) One note = one test If you need multiple tests, make separate notes with different prompts. 3) Use strong stems Allowed stems: - “Define …” - “Purpose of …” - “Difference between … and …” - “If … then what?” - “Which category/quadrant/step applies when …?” - “What comes next after …?” (only if order matters) Banned stems: - “The key is …” - “It is important to …” - “Remember that …” - Long quotes/paragraphs on the front 4) Include disambiguation Always include the framework name or a unique label on the front. 5) Answer length Answer should be: - ideally <12 words - or a short bullet list (max 3 bullets) 6) Add context on the back One line: why it matters, or common confusion. 7) Cap cards per concept Max 5 cards per concept unless it’s truly foundational. 8) Review your cards immediately After creating 10 cards, preview them and ask: “Would future me understand this question?” If you follow this, you won’t recreate the flood.
This is exactly what I needed. I’m still overwhelmed but it feels manageable. One more question: should I use cloze for acronyms? I have some acronym-based lists.

Acronyms can work well if they’re stable and you genuinely want to recall them. Best approach: - One card to expand the acronym (recognize → recall) Front: “Expand C-P-O-R-D.” Back: “Collect, Process, Organize, Review, Do.” Then separate cards for meaning: Front: “In that process, what happens in ‘Process’?” Back: “Clarify items into outcomes/next actions; decide what they are.” Cloze is okay for acronym expansions, but keep it to one cloze: “C-P-O-R-D = Collect, Process, Organize, Review, {{c1::Do}}.” But Basic is still cleaner. Also, acronyms can become empty if you don’t connect them to actions. So always add at least a couple of “meaning” cards.
Okay. I’m going to keep going with the plan. If I come back later with stats, can you help interpret them?

Yes. Bring: - Reviews/day trend (last 30 days) - True retention (if available) or Again rate - Average time per review - Number of mature cards - Any deck option values you changed Then we can decide: - Whether to increase new cards - Whether to adjust steps - Whether to consider FSRS - Where your workload is coming from (new vs lapses vs leeches)
Thanks. I already feel less trapped. I’ll keep new at 0 for another week and keep suspending junk.

Good. Keep the minimum daily habit, protect your attention, and treat your deck as a living system you maintain—not a machine that punishes you. When you’re ready to reintroduce new cards, start small and enforce the style guide.
